schools.summary: God has made the Ephesians spiritually alive — 'quickened (made alive, the verb supplied from v.5)' those who were lifeless in sin; spiritual life is God's gift; they were 'dead in trespasses (paraptoma — fallings, transgressions) and sins (hamartia)' — not merely sick or weak, but dead, spiritually lifeless, helpless and unresponsive toward God; the strongest statement of human depravity (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Henry): spiritual death means total inability to quicken oneself; as a corpse cannot raise itself, so the dead in sin must be made alive by God's power alone.

themes: And you hath he quickened, who were dead in trespasses and sins
the spiritually dead made alive
dead in trespasses and sins
total inability of the dead

schools.summary: In their dead state the Ephesians 'walked (lived, conducted themselves) according to the course (aion — the age, the spirit and fashion) of this world' — following the world's sinful customs and values; 'according to the prince of the power of the air' — Satan, the ruler of the evil powers (pictured as in the air, the lower heavens), prince of demons who holds sway over the disobedient; 'the spirit that now worketh (is energetically at work) in the children of disobedience' — Satan actively operating in those who refuse to obey God (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: ye walked according to the course of this world, according to the prince of the power of the air
the spirit that now worketh in the children of disobedience
the former walk under the world and Satan
the prince of the power of the air

schools.summary: Paul includes himself and all ('we all'): they had their 'conversation (manner of life) in times past in the lusts of our flesh, fulfilling the desires (wills) of the flesh and of the mind' — gratifying both bodily appetites and the corrupt inclinations of the mind; 'and were by nature (physei — by birth, by our natural condition) the children of wrath, even as others (the rest)' — by nature, not merely by act, all are objects of God's wrath, deserving condemnation, Jew and Gentile alike (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: a key text for original sin and native depravity — we are 'by nature' the children of wrath, objects of condemnation from birth, not merely by individual acts; none is exempt, all sharing the same fallen nature inherited from Adam.

themes: we all had our conversation in times past in the lusts of our flesh
were by nature the children of wrath, even as others
all by nature children of wrath
native depravity

schools.summary: The glorious turn ('but God') that breaks into the dark picture of human ruin: 'God, who is rich (wealthy, abounding) in mercy' — over against man's deadness and wrath stands God's abundant mercy, the hinge of the passage; 'for his great love wherewith he loved us' — it was God's own great love, freely set on the unlovely and dead, that moved him to act; love and mercy, not any merit in us, are the cause (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Henry): that God should love and have mercy on those who were dead in sin and children of wrath is the marvel of grace; the 'but God' turns the whole from ruin to salvation.

themes: But God, who is rich in mercy, for his great love wherewith he loved us
God rich in mercy and great love
the great turn from ruin to grace
love freely set on the dead

schools.summary: 'Even when we were dead in sins (at the very point of our deadness, not after we improved) hath quickened us together with Christ (synezoopoiesen)' — made us alive together with the risen Christ, our new life flowing from union with him; believers are quickened 'together with Christ,' their spiritual resurrection bound up with his — as he was raised, so they are raised to new life in him (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Calvin): Paul breaks in with the great parenthesis — 'by grace ye are saved' — unable to mention this quickening without ascribing it wholly to grace; salvation is entirely of free favour, not of works.

themes: even when we were dead in sins, hath quickened us together with Christ
by grace ye are saved
the dead quickened together with Christ
new life by grace in union with Christ

schools.summary: God has 'raised us up together (synegeiren — co-raised)' with Christ and 'made us sit together (synekathisen — co-seated) in heavenly places in Christ Jesus' — believers share in Christ's resurrection and his exaltation, by virtue of union with him; this is, in a sense, already true: in Christ their head, believers are already raised and seated in the heavenly realm, what is true of the head reckoned true of the members, the future glory guaranteed in him (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): the believer's exaltation with Christ is begun in spiritual life and assured of consummation; the seating with him in heavenly places is the pledge of final glory.

themes: hath raised us up together, and made us sit together in heavenly places in Christ Jesus
believers raised and seated with Christ
sharing Christ's resurrection and exaltation
the pledge of final glory

schools.summary: God's purpose in saving and exalting believers: 'that in the ages to come (the endless future) he might shew (display, exhibit) the exceeding (surpassing) riches of his grace' — believers are an eternal exhibition of God's grace; shown 'in his kindness (goodness) toward us through Christ Jesus' — God's saving kindness, displayed in the redeemed, channelled through Christ; the saved are the trophies of his grace forever (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: that in the ages to come he might shew the exceeding riches of his grace
in his kindness toward us through Christ Jesus
the eternal display of God's grace
the redeemed as trophies of grace

schools.summary: 'By grace ye are saved (have been and continue saved) through faith' — grace is the source and moving cause (God's part), faith the instrument by which it is received (our part). 'And that (touto, neuter) not of yourselves: it is the gift of God' — the neuter 'that' does not grammatically agree with 'faith' (pistis, feminine), and so refers not to faith specifically but to the whole act/fact of salvation by grace through faith; this salvation is God's gift, not of ourselves (the view of Calvin, Storr, Locke, Grotius, and most). Barnes adds that faith is, in another sense, God's gift — the inclination produced by the Spirit — though the act of believing is the believer's own (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Clarke: the gift is salvation, not faith (the neuter 'this' has the whole foregoing sentence for its antecedent); God gives the grace and power to believe, but the act of believing is man's own — without the power none can believe, but with it any man may, which is why Scripture warns and threatens those who do not. Wesley: 'that ye are saved through faith' is the gift of God.

A minority (Chrysostom, Theodoret, with Beza and Doddridge) refer 'that' to faith — 'faith is the gift of God'; but Theophylact and most take it of being saved by faith, not faith itself, as the gift.

debates.question: What is 'the gift of God' — faith specifically, or salvation by grace as a whole?

sides.position: The whole salvation by grace through faith (the neuter 'that' cannot grammatically refer to 'faith,' feminine)
Faith specifically is the gift of God

themes: by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God
salvation by grace through faith
the gift of God
the grammatical crux of the gift

schools.summary: Paul's emphatic negative: salvation is 'not of works' — not procured, merited, or earned by any human works of any kind; the door to works-righteousness is wholly shut; God's design being 'lest any man should boast (glory)' — that no one may have ground to boast in himself, for if works could save, man would have something to glory in, but grace excludes all boasting (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: Paul excludes not merely ceremonial works (as the Papists claimed) but all works, the whole righteousness of man; the contrast of God and man, grace and works, leaves boasting no place — the whole praise of salvation belongs to God's mercy alone.

themes: Not of works, lest any man should boast
salvation not of works
no room for boasting
grace excluding all merit

schools.summary: The ground of the foregoing: 'we are his workmanship (poiema — his making, his handiwork), created (a new creation) in Christ Jesus' — believers are wholly God's work, made new in Christ, salvation God's creative act not man's achievement; 'created unto good works' — good works are not the cause of salvation but its purpose and fruit (we are saved not by good works but for them); 'which God hath before ordained (prepared beforehand) that we should walk in them' — the life of holiness itself prepared and appointed by God (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Calvin): grace produces the very works it does not depend on; God prepared beforehand the good works in which believers walk, so that holiness is the appointed fruit of the new creation, all of God.

themes: we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus unto good works
which God hath before ordained that we should walk in them
believers as God's workmanship
created unto good works

schools.summary: Paul bids the Gentile believers 'remember' their former condition: they were 'Gentiles in the flesh' — by birth and outward state outside the covenant people; the Jews ('the Circumcision') contemptuously called the Gentiles 'Uncircumcision' (a term of reproach), though their own circumcision was merely 'in the flesh, made by hands' — an outward, manual rite, not the inward circumcision of the heart (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Henry: Paul would have them recall their former alienation, that they may the more value the grace that brought them near; remembering the past magnifies the mercy of the present.

themes: ye being in time past Gentiles in the flesh, who are called Uncircumcision
by that which is called the Circumcision in the flesh made by hands
the Gentiles' former state
remembering their alienation

schools.summary: The dark catalogue of the Gentiles' former state: 'without Christ (having no Messiah, no Saviour), being aliens from the commonwealth (the citizenship, the polity) of Israel' (outside God's covenant nation), 'and strangers from the covenants of promise' (having no part in the covenants by which God promised redemption), 'having no hope (no well-founded expectation of future blessedness), and without God (atheoi — godless, having no true God) in the world' — the depth of Gentile misery, hopeless and Godless (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom: the climax of the Gentiles' lostness is to be without hope and without God in the world; theirs was the deepest misery, from which the grace of Christ alone could deliver.

themes: ye were without Christ, being aliens from the commonwealth of Israel, and strangers from the covenants of promise
having no hope, and without God in the world
the Gentiles' fivefold former lostness
without hope and without God

schools.summary: The glorious turn ('but now') matching the 'but God' of v.4: 'in Christ Jesus ye who sometimes (formerly) were far off (distant from God, the covenant, and hope — the rabbinic term for those outside the covenant) are made nigh (brought near)' — the whole former distance reversed in Christ, the Gentiles brought into nearness to God and into his people (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: this nearness is 'by the blood of Christ' — his atoning death is the price and means by which the far-off are brought near; reconciliation is through the cross, not by any approach of our own.

themes: But now in Christ Jesus ye who sometimes were far off are made nigh by the blood of Christ
the far-off Gentiles brought near
made nigh by the blood of Christ
reconciliation by the cross

schools.summary: 'He (Christ) is our peace' — not merely the maker but himself the peace — 'who hath made both (Jew and Gentile) one' — uniting the two long-divided peoples into one body; 'and hath broken down the middle wall of partition (the dividing wall, the fence)' — the barrier that separated Jew from Gentile, the image drawn from the wall in the temple that shut Gentiles out of the inner courts, signifying the ceremonial law that divided them (Barnes, Robertson).
Henry: by removing the dividing barrier, Christ made peace between the two and reconciled both to God; he is the bond and the substance of their unity, our peace in person.

themes: he is our peace, who hath made both one
hath broken down the middle wall of partition between us
Christ our peace breaking the middle wall
uniting Jew and Gentile

schools.summary: Christ 'abolished in his flesh (by his death) the enmity, even the law of commandments contained in ordinances' — the ceremonial law, with its rites and observances, which had been the dividing barrier between Jew and Gentile; this Christ annulled; his design 'to make in himself of twain (the two, Jew and Gentile) one new man' — not Jew absorbing Gentile or the reverse, but both fused into a wholly 'new man,' a new humanity in Christ — 'so making peace' (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the ceremonial law, the wall of separation, is abolished; the two are made one new man, a new creation in Christ, in whom the old distinction of Jew and Gentile is done away and peace established.

themes: Having abolished in his flesh the enmity, even the law of commandments contained in ordinances
to make in himself of twain one new man, so making peace
the law of ordinances abolished
one new man made

schools.summary: The second design: 'that he might reconcile both (Jew and Gentile) unto God in one body by the cross' — not only uniting the two to each other but reconciling both, now one body (the church), to God himself, through the cross; their union with each other and their reconciliation to God bound together (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: 'having slain the enmity (both between the two and toward God) thereby (by the cross)' — the cross put the enmity to death, abolishing both the division between Jew and Gentile and the alienation of both from God.

themes: that he might reconcile both unto God in one body by the cross
having slain the enmity thereby
both reconciled to God in one body
the enmity slain by the cross

schools.summary: Christ 'came and preached (proclaimed, announced — by his coming, his Spirit, and his apostles) peace to you which were afar off (the Gentiles, distant from the covenant) and to them that were nigh (the Jews, who had the covenant)' — the gospel of peace announced to both, the echo of Isaiah 57:19, the same peace to both, none excluded (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

themes: came and preached peace to you which were afar off, and to them that were nigh
Christ preaching peace to far and near
the gospel of peace to Gentile and Jew
peace proclaimed to both

schools.summary: 'Through him (Christ) we both (Jew and Gentile) have access (prosagoge — the right of approach, introduction, as into a king's presence)' — Christ is the one way of approach to God for both peoples; the threefold structure 'through him (the Son), by one Spirit (the Holy Spirit), unto the Father' a clear trinitarian witness, and the 'one Spirit' marking the unity, both having the same access by the same Spirit (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the once-distant Gentiles now share with the Jews the highest privilege — free access to God as Father, through the Son and by the Spirit; the verse displays the work of the whole Trinity in the believer's approach to God.

themes: through him we both have access by one Spirit unto the Father
access to the Father through Christ by one Spirit
the trinitarian way of approach
Jew and Gentile united in access

schools.summary: The conclusion ('now therefore'): the Gentiles are 'no more strangers (xenoi — aliens) and foreigners (paroikoi — resident aliens, sojourners without civic rights)' — their former alienation (v.12) ended; now 'fellowcitizens with the saints' — full citizens of God's commonwealth, sharing equal rights with all the people of God; and 'of the household (the family) of God' — members of God's own family, with the intimacy and privileges of children in his house (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: ye are no more strangers and foreigners, but fellowcitizens with the saints
and of the household of God
the Gentiles now fellow-citizens and family
enfranchised and adopted

schools.summary: The figure shifts to a building: believers are 'built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets' — either the foundation that the apostles and prophets laid (their doctrine, the gospel they preached), or the foundation which they themselves are; the 'prophets' being most likely the New Testament prophets (Ellicott, Robertson), or by others the Old Testament prophets; and 'Jesus Christ himself being the chief corner stone (akrogoniaios)' — the stone that binds the two walls (Jew and Gentile) together and supports the whole, fitly picturing Christ uniting the two into one temple (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Christ is the foundation and bond of the whole building; the corner stone joins and sustains the entire structure, so that the church stands and coheres only in him.

themes: built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets
Jesus Christ himself being the chief corner stone
the church built on the apostolic foundation
Christ the corner stone

schools.summary: 'In whom (in Christ) all the building, fitly framed together (synarmologoumene — joined and fitted, each stone in its place) groweth (auxei — grows, as a living organism)' — the church is a building that, joined in Christ, grows like a living thing; 'unto an holy temple (naos — a sanctuary, the dwelling-place of God) in the Lord' — becoming a holy temple, consecrated to God, in union with the Lord, the goal of the growth being to be God's sanctuary (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the fitting-together and growth happen 'in him' and 'in the Lord' — Christ is the principle of both the cohesion and the increase of the temple; the church coheres and grows only in union with him.

themes: all the building fitly framed together groweth unto an holy temple in the Lord
the building growing into a holy temple
the church a living, growing temple
fitly framed in Christ

schools.summary: The application to the Gentile believers: 'in whom (in Christ) ye also (the Gentiles) are builded together (synoikodomeisthe — built up together with the rest)' — incorporated into the same building, fellow-stones in God's temple; 'for an habitation (katoiketerion — a dwelling-place, a permanent residence) of God through (by, in) the Spirit' — the church is the temple in which God dwells, not by outward symbols but by his Spirit; God inhabits his people through the Holy Spirit, the once-far-off Gentiles now the very dwelling-place of God (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the church is God's habitation by the Spirit — he dwells in his people, the living temple, through the Holy Ghost; this is the highest dignity, that God should make his abode in the once-alien Gentiles together with the Jews.

themes: in whom ye also are builded together for an habitation of God through the Spirit
the Gentiles built together as God's dwelling
the church God's Spirit-indwelt temple
God dwelling in his people by the Spirit
